experiment: TokensTransfer (LLMLingua-2) vs compression baselines — measured comparative benchmark
battery: 8 synthetic-but-realistic English documents (2 meeting transcripts, 2 technical docs, 2 news-style articles, 2 code-review discussions), 300-540 words each, 6 hand-picked exact-substring facts per document
facts_retention_methodology_caveat: facts_retained is a crude proxy: exact substring match (whitespace-normalized) of the original fact string against the compressed output. It does NOT credit paraphrases, partial matches, or numbers/entities expressed differently (e.g. '$1.2 million' vs '1.2M dollars'). Extractive methods (LLMLingua-2 token-level, TextRank/LexRank sentence-level, truncation, random sampling) all preserve original wording, so this proxy is fair BETWEEN these five methods, but would undercount any abstractive/paraphrasing method if one were added.

Sliding window logs were rejected due to memory overhead — storing a timestamp per request for high-volume keys would require roughly 800 MB per million active keys, which exceeds our Redis budget. We selected the token bucket algorithm implemented via a Redis Lua script for atomicity, which requires only 24 bytes of state per key.
